content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 
1102 Q Street• Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Angela Brand 
221 N. Cirby Way 
Roseville, CA 95661 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-19-227 
Dear Ms. Brand: 
December 10, 2019 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the post-government employment 
provisions of the Political Reform Act ("Act"). 1 Please note that we are only providing advice under 
the post-government employment provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the 
application, if any, of other post government employment laws, such as Public Contract Code 
section 10411. 
Also, we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 
71); any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if 
the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice. 
QUESTIONS 
As a former employee of the Mental Health Services Oversight and Accountability 
Commission ("MHSOAC"), may you participate in the following under the one-year ban or the 
permanent ban: 
1) 
Attend and participate at meetings where MHSOAC staff or Commissioners are present? 
2) 
Utilize your expertise gleaned from the MHSOAC in advising the California branch of the 
National Alliance on Mental Health ("NAMI") staff that might appear before the MHSOAC? 
3) 
Contract implementation and compliance ifNAMI were awarded new grants with the 
MHSOAC following competitive bidding of its 2020 contracts? 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through I 8997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


CONCLUSIONS 
File No. A-19-227 
Page No. 2 
The one-year ban is applicable to you and prohibits you as an employee of a private 
company from communicating or appearing before the MHSOAC to influence any administrative, 
legislative, or discretionary acts for one year from your date of permanent separation from the 
MHSOAC. The permanent ban prohibits you from appearing or communicating in proceedings 
involving specific parties, such as the grant and contract proceedings in which you were involved 
during your employment. Specifically, 
1) 
You may attend and participate at general meetings involving the MHSOAC to the extent 
that your attendance and participation at these public meetings is not for the purpose of influencing 
any administrative or legislative actions or any discretionary act involving the issuance, 
amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase 
of goods or property. Generally, as long as the decision is not before the MHSO~C, you may attend 
and participate in the meeting so long as you do not use the opportunity to make an appearance or 
communication for the purpose of influencing a decision before the MHSOAC. Due to your 
involvement in the two 2016-2017 MHSOAC contracts in the formation and implementation stages, 
you are subject to the permanent ban regarding the two existing contracts. You may not participate 
in any meetings or communications regarding the contracts, 
2) 
Generally, advising individuals who might appear before or communicate with the 
MHSOAC is not a prohibited appearance or communication under the one-year ban so long as you 
are not identified in connection with the appearance or communication, and it is not prohibited 
under the permanent ban. Regarding the permanent ban, you are prohibited from advising any party 
other than the state related to MHSOAC contracts that you participated in during the formation or 
implementation process while employed at the MHSOAC. 
3) 
The permanent ban prohibits you from participating in ongoing activities with MHSOAC 
contracts that you personally and substantially participated in as a former MHSOAC employee, 
such as MHSOAC's 2016-2017 round of grant issuance. However, MHSOAC's 2020 round of 
grant issuance and contracts constitute a new proceeding for purposes of the permanent ban. 
FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
You were employed by the Mental Health Services Oversight and Accountability 
Commission ("MHSOAC") from January 12, 2015 through April 30, 2019 as a Health Program 
Specialist. This position required you to annually file a Form 700. The MHSOAC administers 
funding through a competitive bid process to statewide non-profit agencies to perform advocacy 
related work. These funds are awarded every three years under separate and unique procurement 
processes led by MHSOAC staff and approved by MHSOAC Commissioners. 
During your employment, you were part of a team that led the development, 
implementation, and monitoring of contracts administered to statewide non-profit agencies focused 
on supporting mental health advocacy. This process included public engagement efforts to establish 
a scope of work for eight contracts and the drafting of documents that were used as part of the 
public competitive bid process released in May 2016. 


File No. A-19-227 
Page No. 3 
As part of this effort, you worked with a team to support the process of issuing the 
procurements for public bid. You were not involved in the selection of the agencies receiving the 
awards; you did not sit on any of the bid scoring panels, and; you were not involved in assigning 
points to any of the received proposals. Upon completion of an independent scoring process, you 
summarized the results for review and approval by internal legal counsel. The summarized results 
were then presented to the Commission for approval. Approval authorized the executive director to 
award grants to eight winning organizations. 
You were assigned as the contract monitor to the eight awarded organizations, receiving 
deliverables and invoices and submitting them for approval to the appropriate staff, your direct 
supervisor, and department deputy director. You facilitated quarterly collaboration meetings 
between all contractors where program managers would meet to discuss upcoming events and 
activities and share lessons learned and challenges. You also worked with each contracted program 
manager to ensure timely completion of work and provide technical assistance and support during 
the contract period which began September 1, 2017 and ends on August 31, 2020. 
On April 30, 2019, you left state service. In October 2019, you were offered a position with 
the California branch of the National Alliance on Mental Illness ("NAMI"), a privately-held mental 
health non-profit organization. NAMI holds a number of contracts from multiple funders, including 
two grants with the MHSOAC. These grants were part of the eight statewide contracts that you 
monitored. As a NAMI employee, you will not be part of any of the efforts to complete any of the 
program work in the two MHSOAC contracts. You will be serving in a capacity outside that 
contracted work. 
You will meet with community partners/organizations and attend their meetings and events, 
where the MHSOAC may be present. At MHSOAC meetings, information is provided about myriad 
projects, including statewide innovation funding plans, budget discussions, informational 
presentations, et cetera. Your attendance and participation will be limited to gathering publicly 
available information. You do not plan to speak to staff or Commissioners, nor will you make any 
form of public comment or written feedback. You will be in attendance as part of an effort to stay 
informed about state level advocacy trends and needs. 
As part of its contractual responsibilities, NAMI staff meet quarterly with MHSOAC staff to 
review progress toward completion of current contract activities as well as to share ideas and 
identify opportunities for collaboration with other contracted non-profit partners. These meetings do 
not involve any amendments to or revisions of contract language. You want to provide general 
information to NAMI staff, outside entities, and community partners/organizations on MHSOAC's 
procedural requirements, plans, and policies regarding administering and monitoring contracts. 
The MHSOAC will release a new round of requests for proposals to solicit competitive bids 
for statewide advocacy activities. The MHSOAC's new process defining the scope of work for the 
next round of funding began after you left the MHSOAC. You have no knowledge of the process 
used by MHSOAC to create the requests for proposals. It is independent of the process and 
requirements that informed the current contract period. 
· 
ANALYSIS 


File No. A-19-227 
Page No. 4 
Public officials who leave state service are subject to two types of post-governmental 
employment provisions under the Act, the one-year ban and the permanent ban. 
One-Year Ban: · 
The "one-year ban" prohibits a former state employee from making, for compensation, any 
formal or informal.appearance, or making any oral or written communication, before his or her 
former agency for the purpose of influencing any administrative or legislative actions or any 
discretionary act involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, 
grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. (Section 87406; Regulation 
18746.1.) 
An appearance or communication is for the "purpose of influencing" if it is made for the 
"principal purpose of supporting, promoting, influencing, modifying, opposing, delaying, or 
advancing the action or proceeding." (Regulation 18746.2(a).) 
You are subject to the one-year ban until April 30, 2020. You may not make a formal or 
informal appearance before the MHSOAC on behalf of a client, for the purpose of influencing any 
administrative or legislative actions or any discretionary acts involving the issuance, amendment, 
awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or 
property during that time period .. You may provide policy review and advice to NAMI staff or other 
third parties, if you are not identified with these efforts in regard to the MHSOAC, and so long as 
you are not otherwise prohibited under the permanent ban. 
Certain communications are not restricted under the one-year ban. A communication is not 
subject to the one-year ban when the former official: 
(1) Participates as a panelist or formal speaker at a conference or similar public event for 
educational purposes or to disseminate research and the subject matter does not pertain to a specific 
' 
. 
action or proceeding; 
(2) Attends a general informational meeting, seminar, or similar event; 
(3) Requests information concerning any matter of public record; or 
(4) Communicates with the press. 
(Regulation 18746.2(b)(l)-(4).) 
Under the one-year ban, you are generally prohibited from making an appearance or 
. communication with the MHSOAC, including any formal or informal appearance or oral or written 
communication, for the purpose of influencing a matter in front of the MHSOAC. (Section 
87406(d)(l).) These communications include, but are not limited to, conversing directly or by 
telephone, corresponding by writing or email, attending a meeting, and delivering or sending any 
communication. (Regulation 18746.2(a).) However, to the extent that you attend general 
informational meetings involving the MHSOAC and your participation as a current employee of 
NAMI is limited to requesting public information, your appearance and communication is not 
prohibited by the one-year ban under the narrow exceptions in Regulation 18746(b), noted above. 


File No. A-19-227 
Page No. 5 
Additionally, you are not prohibited from attending and participating in meetings involving 
a decision not before the MHSOAC merely because MHSOAC officials are attending the same 
meeting. The one-year ban generally does not apply to a proceeding in which the former state 
employee's state agency is not the governmental decision-maker in the proceeding. (See Chan 
Advice Letter, No. I-09-063.) Therefore, your appearance or a communication in a meeting before 
another decision-making agency is not generally prohibited. However, we caution that you may not 
use the occasion to attempt to influence your former state employer in any of its decisions relating 
to its own administrative or legislative actions or in its, decisions to issue, amend, or revoke a 
license, grant, contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property, and that you may still be 
prohibited from attending or participating if the permanent ban applies. 
In regard to whether you can utilize your MHSOAC experience in advising NAM! 
colleagues, outside entities, and community partners on MHSOAC general procedural 
requirements, plans, and policies for administering contracts and monitoring work, we have 
previously advised that so long .:is not otherwise prohibited by the permanent ban, a former agency 
official may use his or her expertise to advise clients on the procedural requirements, plans, or 
policies of his or her former agency, so long as the former employee is not identified in the client's 
efforts to influence the agency. (See, for example, Perry Advice Letter, No. A-94-004.) To the 
extent that your communications are not with your former agency nor prohibited under the 
permanent ban, as discussed below, advising on MHSOAC general procedures and policies will not 
violate the one-year ban. 
Permanent Ban: 
The "permanent ban" prohibits a former state employee from "switching sides" and 
participating, for compensation, in a certain proceeding involving the State of California and other 
specific parties or assisting in the proceeding if the proceeding is one in which the former state 
employee participated while employed by the state. (Sections 87401 and 87402; Regulation 
18741.1.) 
The permanent ban is a lifetime ban and applies to any formal or informal appearance or any 
oral or written communication - or aiding, advising, counseling, consulting, or assisting in 
representing any other person, other than the State of California, in an appearance or 
communication - made with the intent to•influence any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding 
in which you participated while you served as a state administrative official. 
'" Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding' means any proceeding, application, request 
for a ruling or other determination, contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation, 
arrest or other particular matter involving a specific party or parties in any court or state 
administrative agency ... " (Section 87400(c).) Additionally, an official is considered to have 
"participated" in a proceeding if he or she took part in the proceeding "personally, and substantially 
through decision, approval, disapproval, formal written recommendation, rendering advice on a 
substantial basis, investigation, or use of confidential information ... " (Section 87400(d).) Given 
your participation in the development, implementation, and monitoring of the MHSOAC's current 
contracts with NAMI, you are subject to the permanent ban regarding the two contracts. You may 
not make any appearances or attend meetings regarding these contracts. Nor may you advise any 
party other than the MHSOAC regarding the two existing contracts. 


New Proceedings: 
File No. A-19-227 
Page No. 6 
The permanent ban does not apply to a "new" proceeding even in cases where the new 
proceeding is related to or grows out of a prior proceeding in which the official had participated. 
(Sections 87401 and 87402.) A "new" proceeding not subject to the permanent ban typically 
involves different parties, a different subject matter, or different factual or legal issues from those 
considered in previous proceedings. 
You intend on attending MHSOAC events for the agency's new request for proposals 
process. MHSOAC's next round of grants are scheduled for July 2020. MHSOAC's contracting 
processes will consist of a whole new set of activities and tasks for each contractor to bid on. Given 
that these future grants are separate and unique from MHSOAC's current grants, the MHSOAC's 
2020 grant process, contract procurement, and c.ompetitive bid procedures are a new proceeding. As 
such, the permanent ban would not prohibit your attendance and participation at these meetings. 
However, so long as you are subject to the one-year ban, you may not attend these meetings for the 
purpose of influencing the MHSOAC nor may you attend if these meetings do not qualify as 
general informational meetings. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
RPOC:aja 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
f',O~ 
Ryan O'Connor 
Counsel, Legal Division

sections.analysis:
Public officials who leave state service are subject to two types of post-governmental 
employment provisions under the Act, the one-year ban and the permanent ban. 
One-Year Ban: · 
The "one-year ban" prohibits a former state employee from making, for compensation, any 
formal or informal.appearance, or making any oral or written communication, before his or her 
former agency for the purpose of influencing any administrative or legislative actions or any 
discretionary act involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, 
grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. (Section 87406; Regulation 
18746.1.) 
An appearance or communication is for the "purpose of influencing" if it is made for the 
"principal purpose of supporting, promoting, influencing, modifying, opposing, delaying, or 
advancing the action or proceeding." (Regulation 18746.2(a).) 
You are subject to the one-year ban until April 30, 2020. You may not make a formal or 
informal appearance before the MHSOAC on behalf of a client, for the purpose of influencing any 
administrative or legislative actions or any discretionary acts involving the issuance, amendment, 
awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or 
property during that time period .. You may provide policy review and advice to NAMI staff or other 
third parties, if you are not identified with these efforts in regard to the MHSOAC, and so long as 
you are not otherwise prohibited under the permanent ban. 
Certain communications are not restricted under the one-year ban. A communication is not 
subject to the one-year ban when the former official: 
(1) Participates as a panelist or formal speaker at a conference or similar public event for 
educational purposes or to disseminate research and the subject matter does not pertain to a specific 
' 
. 
action or proceeding; 
(2) Attends a general informational meeting, seminar, or similar event; 
(3) Requests information concerning any matter of public record; or 
(4) Communicates with the press. 
(Regulation 18746.2(b)(l)-(4).) 
Under the one-year ban, you are generally prohibited from making an appearance or 
. communication with the MHSOAC, including any formal or informal appearance or oral or written 
communication, for the purpose of influencing a matter in front of the MHSOAC. (Section 
87406(d)(l).) These communications include, but are not limited to, conversing directly or by 
telephone, corresponding by writing or email, attending a meeting, and delivering or sending any 
communication. (Regulation 18746.2(a).) However, to the extent that you attend general 
informational meetings involving the MHSOAC and your participation as a current employee of 
NAMI is limited to requesting public information, your appearance and communication is not 
prohibited by the one-year ban under the narrow exceptions in Regulation 18746(b), noted above.  
Additionally, you are not prohibited from attending and participating in meetings involving 
a decision not before the MHSOAC merely because MHSOAC officials are attending the same 
meeting. The one-year ban generally does not apply to a proceeding in which the former state 
employee's state agency is not the governmental decision-maker in the proceeding. (See Chan 
Advice Letter, No. I-09-063.) Therefore, your appearance or a communication in a meeting before 
another decision-making agency is not generally prohibited. However, we caution that you may not 
use the occasion to attempt to influence your former state employer in any of its decisions relating 
to its own administrative or legislative actions or in its, decisions to issue, amend, or revoke a 
license, grant, contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property, and that you may still be 
prohibited from attending or participating if the permanent ban applies. 
In regard to whether you can utilize your MHSOAC experience in advising NAM! 
colleagues, outside entities, and community partners on MHSOAC general procedural 
requirements, plans, and policies for administering contracts and monitoring work, we have 
previously advised that so long .:is not otherwise prohibited by the permanent ban, a former agency 
official may use his or her expertise to advise clients on the procedural requirements, plans, or 
policies of his or her former agency, so long as the former employee is not identified in the client's 
efforts to influence the agency. (See, for example, Perry Advice Letter, No. A-94-004.) To the 
extent that your communications are not with your former agency nor prohibited under the 
permanent ban, as discussed below, advising on MHSOAC general procedures and policies will not 
violate the one-year ban. 
Permanent Ban: 
The "permanent ban" prohibits a former state employee from "switching sides" and 
participating, for compensation, in a certain proceeding involving the State of California and other 
specific parties or assisting in the proceeding if the proceeding is one in which the former state 
employee participated while employed by the state. (Sections 87401 and 87402; Regulation 
18741.1.) 
The permanent ban is a lifetime ban and applies to any formal or informal appearance or any 
oral or written communication - or aiding, advising, counseling, consulting, or assisting in 
representing any other person, other than the State of California, in an appearance or 
communication - made with the intent to•influence any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding 
in which you participated while you served as a state administrative official. 
'" Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding' means any proceeding, application, request 
for a ruling or other determination, contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation, 
arrest or other particular matter involving a specific party or parties in any court or state

through decision, approval, disapproval, formal written recommendation, rendering advice on a

contracts with NAMI, you are subject to the permanent ban regarding the two contracts. You may 
not make any appearances or attend meetings regarding these contracts. Nor may you advise any 
party other than the MHSOAC regarding the two existing contracts. 


New Proceedings:  
The permanent ban does not apply to a "new" proceeding even in cases where the new 
proceeding is related to or grows out of a prior proceeding in which the official had participated. 
(Sections 87401 and 87402.) A "new" proceeding not subject to the permanent ban typically 
involves different parties, a different subject matter, or different factual or legal issues from those 
considered in previous proceedings. 
You intend on attending MHSOAC events for the agency's new request for proposals

embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION · 1102 Q Street• Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 Angela Brand 221 N. Cirby Way Roseville, CA 95661 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-19-227 Dear Ms. Brand: December 10, 2019 This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the post-government employment provisions of the Political Reform Act ("Act"). 1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the post-government employment provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other post government employment laws, such as Public Contract Code section 10411. Also, we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71); any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice. QUESTIONS As a former employee of the Mental Health Services Oversight and Accountability Commission ("MHSOAC"), may you participate in the following under the one-year ban or the permanent ban: 1) Attend and participate at meetings where MHSOAC staff or Commissioners are present? 2) Utilize your expertise gleaned from the MHSOAC in advising the California branch of the National Alliance on Mental Health ("NAMI") staff that might appear before the MHSOAC? 3) Contract implementation and compliance ifNAMI were awarded new grants with the MHSOAC following competitive bidding of its 2020 contracts? 1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through I 8997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. CONCLUSIONS File No. A-19-227 Page No. 2 The one-year ban is applicable to you and prohibits you as an employee of a private company from communicating or appearing before the MHSOAC to influence any administrative, legislative, or discretionary acts for one year from your date of permanent separation from the MHSOAC. The permanent ban prohibits you from appearing or communicating in proceedings involving specific parties, such as the grant and contract proceedings in which you were involved during your employment. Specifically, 1) You may attend and participate at general meetings involving the MHSOAC to the extent that your attendance and participation at these public meetings is not for the purpose of influencing any administrative or legislative actions or any discretionary act involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. Generally, as long as the decision is not before the MHSO~C, you may attend and participate in the meeting so long as you do not use the opportunity to make an appearance or communication for the purpose of influencing a
